episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Technology shouldn't sacrifice the integrity of knowledge and Seattle University School of Law Hosts Conference on Indigenous Perspectives on AI, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When technology enables discovery of concealed evidence (like residential school graves), how should communities balance the capacity to surface knowledge with governance frameworks that protect how that knowledge is used and disclosed? Topics include: Data sovereignty and community consent in residential school investigations, Technology capabilities (GPR, LiDAR) vs. governance frameworks for knowledge use, Federal funding architecture and its role as both enabler and gatekeeper, Academic research partnerships and data storage/publication control, AI risk to Indigenous knowledge in publicly accessible datasets.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Aboriginal Training Employment Centre

discussion.central_question: When technology enables discovery of concealed evidence (like residential school graves), how should communities balance the capacity to surface knowledge with governance frameworks that protect how that knowledge is used and disclosed?
discussion.riley_position: Communities should be pragmatic about deploying imperfect technical tools under time pressure, because waiting for perfect legal frameworks means families wait longer for answers; the key is building data governance infrastructure alongside the survey work.
riley_key_evidence: GPR technology allows surveying without excavation, which respects Indigenous legal traditions that consider disturbance a violation
Williams Lake First Nation's documented, deliberate approach to ground surveys and archival research demonstrates sovereignty in practice
FirstVoices model shows community-controlled data repositories with access restrictions can be built to protect knowledge
discussion.casey_position: The pattern of deploying technical infrastructure before governance architecture is settled creates long-term problems; retrofitting governance onto existing systems is harder than building it in from the start, and external pressure to move quickly compounds the risk.
casey_key_evidence: Tk'emlúps te Secwépemc's 2021 GPR announcement went global within hours, politicizing the findings in ways the community didn't control
Federal government funding relationships are not neutral when the government's predecessor institutions ran the schools themselves
AI training datasets could absorb research findings before communities decide whether to make them public—a current risk, not a hypothetical one
discussion.resolution: They agreed that both urgency and governance gaps are real and coexist; they converged on concrete next steps: data storage questions must be answered in writing before partnership agreements, communities should engage the First Nations Technology Council early, and regional institutions should provide administrative capacity in a support role rather than assuming leadership.
topics_covered: Data sovereignty and community consent in residential school investigations
Technology capabilities (GPR, LiDAR) vs. governance frameworks for knowledge use
Federal funding architecture and its role as both enabler and gatekeeper
Academic research partnerships and data storage/publication control
